takeaways: Senior housing M&A heats up: LCS-Vi merger creates a 130-community operator while NHI offloads a $560M portfolio to NHC, sharpening focus on private-pay seniors housing.
Capital is back: Colliers forecasts 15–20% CRE sales-volume growth in 2026 with investment activity rising 16% to $562B and cap rates compressing 5–15 bps as banks return to lending.

AI shifts from experiment to operator-grade tooling — agentic AI now automates leasing, maintenance and fraud-detection workflows even as 12,000+ FBI real estate fraud complaints highlight the downside.

items.headline: LCS-Vi merger closes, creating 130-community senior living operator
items.summary: LCS and Vi finalized their planned merger, with LCS now managing 130 communities across 29 states. The deal positions LCS as one of the largest U.S. operators and signals continued consolidation pressure on mid-size senior living owners — a competitive backdrop Lloyd Jones should track for both acquisition and management opportunities.

items.headline: NHI announces $560M portfolio sale to National HealthCare Corporation
items.summary: NHI agreed to sell a $560M portfolio to National HealthCare Corporation, accelerating its capital recycling strategy and tilting the REIT decisively toward private-pay senior living. The move underscores institutional appetite for private-pay assets and a willingness to shed skilled-nursing exposure — a useful read on cap-rate signals for stabilized properties.

items.headline: Brookdale, now at 'fighting weight,' shifts from defense to growth
items.summary: Brookdale executives say the operator is past its restructuring phase, with portfolio occupancy and margins on an upward trajectory in 2026 and management ready to play offense. Improving fundamentals at the largest U.S. operator are a leading indicator for sector-wide rate growth and operating leverage.

items.headline: Senior housing supply gap to hit 400,000 units by 2035
items.summary: Demand for senior housing is on track to exceed supply by more than 100,000 units in 2027, widening to roughly 400,000 units by 2035 even as new development remains constrained by costs and capital. University-partnership projects continue to outperform — Edenwald/Goucher's Baltimore community sold out in seven weeks — pointing to an under-supplied market with strong lease-up velocity for differentiated product.

items.headline: CRE investment volume forecast to jump 16% to $562B in 2026
items.summary: Colliers and Deloitte project CRE sales volumes rising 15–20% with total investment reaching $562B, nearly matching the 2015–19 pre-pandemic average. Cap rates are expected to compress 5–15 bps across most property types as institutional and cross-border capital re-engage — supportive backdrop for refis and dispositions in 2H 2026.

items.headline: Fed funds at 3.50–3.75%; CRE loan rates 4.99–12.75% as banks return
items.summary: Commercial loan rates as of early May 2026 span 4.99–12.75% with conventional CRE mortgages at 5.25–8.75%, against a Fed target of 3.50–3.75% and the 10-year Treasury near 4.3%. Banks are reemerging as debt providers as the curve steepens, expanding liquidity options beyond debt funds and CMBS for senior housing borrowers.

items.headline: Senior living capital reopens as sector-specific lenders re-engage
items.summary: Multi-Housing News reports senior living capital markets are reopening in 2026 with demand leading and lenders selectively rebuilding pipelines, particularly for stabilized private-pay assets. Equity interest is rising for value-add and lease-up plays — a window for Lloyd Jones to recapitalize and place new acquisition equity.

items.headline: Sterling: Q2 2026 marks return of 'capital discipline' to CRE
items.summary: Sterling Asset Group's Q2 2026 outlook frames the cycle as one of stabilization and selectivity, with capital flowing back but discipline replacing the indiscriminate underwriting of 2021–22. Sponsors with track records and clean basis assumptions will win deals; bid/ask spreads continue to narrow on quality assets.

items.headline: Agentic AI named the next wave of proptech in 2026
items.summary: ICSC and MRI report that agentic AI — systems that autonomously execute multi-step workflows — is the defining proptech trend of 2026, automating leasing, maintenance dispatch, and lease administration end-to-end. Operators piloting agentic stacks are reporting double-digit reductions in back-office FTE load, a margin lever worth evaluating for senior living back-of-house functions.

items.headline: Proptech turns AI on AI: fraud-fighting tools surge as losses top $275M
items.summary: Commercial Observer reports that owners and proptech firms are deploying AI-driven identity verification and document forensics after the FBI logged 12,000+ real estate fraud complaints totaling $275M+ in 2025. Senior housing operators handling deposit and family-payor flows are a prime target — leasing and AR systems should be reviewed for AI-fraud resilience.

items.headline: Proptech valuations rebound as 'monster rounds' return to AI-first platforms
items.summary: Inman and PwC note residential proptech is back in focus with investors paying premium multiples for scaled AI-native platforms — a reset after the 2023–24 funding winter. Capital is concentrating in winners with proven distribution, raising the cost and speed at which dominant operating-system vendors will lock in real estate workflows.

items.headline: Propurti launches AI property management platform May 9
items.summary: Edmonton-based Propurti Geeks officially launches an AI-powered property management OS at MacEwan University on May 9, covering leasing, applications, rent collection, maintenance, communications, and compliance. Adds to a crowded field of integrated AI suites worth scanning during 2026 vendor reviews.

items.headline: DDI: 71% of leaders report rising stress, 40% considering quitting
items.summary: DDI's Global Leadership Forecast 2025 finds 71% of leaders reporting increased role stress and 40% of those weighing exit, against a backdrop of U.S. layoffs at a 10-year high and flatter org structures. Retention of senior site and regional leadership in senior living — already a tight talent pool — is a heightened operational risk for 2026.

items.headline: IESE rebuilds flagship MBA around human-AI system leadership
items.summary: IESE Business School announced a redesigned MBA launching September 2026 focused on leading human–AI teams and decision-making under technological uncertainty. Signals where executive talent is being trained — and what skills boards will increasingly expect from senior operators and asset managers.

items.headline: 2026 leadership playbook: flatter orgs, human + AI roles, sustainability discipline
items.summary: World Business Forum and Center for Leadership Studies converge on five 2026 themes: human + AI co-leadership, flatter hierarchies, addressing burnout and 'quiet cracking,' stalled internal mobility, and the discipline to keep sustainability commitments after the headlines fade. Useful framing for 2026 management offsites and succession planning.

items.headline: Thinkers50 names Best New Management Books for 2026
items.summary: Thinkers50's 2026 list highlights titles on platform strategy, leadership resilience, and innovation at scale — emphasizing adaptability, courage, and systemic thinking. A useful curated reading list for executive teams seeking outside perspective.

items.headline: Wellhub: 89% of employees perform better when wellbeing is prioritized
items.summary: Wellhub's State of Work-Life-Wellness 2026 finds 89% of workers link prioritized wellbeing to higher performance, framing wellness as a productivity lever rather than a perk. For senior living operators, the data strengthens the case for staff-wellness investment as a retention and quality tool.

items.headline: Global Wellness Summit: 'emotional fitness' emerges as new health category
items.summary: GWS's 2026 trend report introduces emotional fitness — training cognitive performance and emotional resilience like physical fitness — as a stand-alone wellness category. Expect programming and amenity expectations to evolve in independent and assisted living settings catering to active adults.

items.headline: Neurowellness and women's-health programming reshape senior wellness offerings
items.summary: GWI and Athletech report that 2026's wellness shift toward neurowellness (brain aging, cognitive resilience) and female-specific physiology dovetails directly with senior living's core demographic. Operators leaning into cognitive health and women's-health programming have a clear merchandising and rate-premium opportunity.

items.headline: Wellness 'third places' rise as new workplace social hubs
items.summary: Research identifies gyms, yoga studios, and wellness hubs as the emerging 'third places' where modern workforces build relationships outside home and office. A reminder that lifestyle programming can be a community-building amenity differentiator — and a partnership opportunity with local wellness operators.
